topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled envelope lay on the coffee table, stark white against the dark wood. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the mug, the ceramic warm but offering no solace. I reread the typed notification for the fifth time. 'Relocating to Seattle.' Seattle. A whole ocean away. A sudden, sharp clench tightened my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and paced the length of the living room. Every surface seemed to mock the sudden, gaping void where my best friend used to be. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the air caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound. "Are you serious?" The words came out tight and brittle, barely above a whisper, yet they carried a heavy weight of disbelief.

[story 2]

I stared at the packed boxes stacked haphazardly in the hallway, the sheer volume of belongings a physical manifestation of the distance that was suddenly expanding between us. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my cheeks. It wasn't just that they were leaving; it was the sheer audacity of it. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself in the familiar sight of the neighborhood. The thought that they could just *decide* to uproot everything, to erase years of shared history with a simple geographical shift, felt deeply unfair. I turned back, my jaw tight. "This isn't even a conversation," I managed, my voice dangerously level, trying to keep the tremor out of it. "You can't just drop this bomb and expect me to just… roll with it."

[story 3]

The phone call had ended moments ago, leaving a ringing silence in its wake that felt deafening. I dropped the receiver onto the cradle with a sharp clatter. My hands were shaking, not from cold, but from a furious, contained energy that vibrated just beneath my skin. I walked into the kitchen, grabbing the nearest glass and turning it over and over in my hands until the condensation made my palms slick. I looked at the map tacked to the corkboard—a dotted line stretching from here to the Pacific Northwest. A wave of profound resentment washed over me, hot and unwelcome. "So, this is it?" I muttered to the empty room, my voice laced with a sharp, controlled bitterness. "You think this is some kind of minor inconvenience? You think I’m just going to sit here and pretend this is okay?"
